title: Names and Countries — Introducing Yourself
objective: Say your name, ask someone's name, say where you come from, and use the origin pattern with มาจาก in a short self-introduction.

teachingFrame.openingHook: Open by asking: what if someone in Thailand asks your name and where you are from? After this lesson, you can answer confidently in Thai.
teachingFrame.scenario: You meet a new Thai person at a café or co-working space. You need to introduce yourself, ask their name, and share where you are from.
teachingFrame.learnerTakeaway: By the end, you can introduce yourself with your name and country of origin in a natural 3–4 line Thai exchange.

sections.heading: Your name in Thai
sections.purpose: Teach ชื่อ (name) and อะไร (what) as individual words, then combine them into the question chunk คุณชื่ออะไร (what is your name?).
spokenNarration: Let's start with two words you will use every time you meet someone new in Thailand.
First word: ชื่อ chûue. Listen first. ชื่อ. This means 'name'. Say it with me: ชื่อ. Notice the falling tone — your voice starts high and drops.
Second word: อะไร à-rai. Listen first. อะไร. This means 'what'. Say it with me: อะไร.
Now we combine them. To ask someone's name politely, you say: คุณชื่ออะไร khun chûue à-rai — what is your name? คุณ means 'you' and adds politeness. Listen again: คุณชื่ออะไร.
In real Thai, you always add a polite ending. Male speakers: คุณชื่ออะไรครับ. Female speakers: คุณชื่ออะไรคะ. Your turn — say the full question with your polite ending before I show the answer.
onScreenBullets: คุณชื่ออะไร | khun chûue à-rai | what is your name?
drills: Listen and repeat: ชื่อ chûue. Again. Now อะไร à-rai. Again. Now the full question: คุณชื่ออะไร.
Pause-and-produce: I want to ask someone their name politely. Say the full question with ครับ or คะ before I show the answer.
Tone echo: ชื่อ has a falling tone — voice goes high then drops. Say ชื่อ three times, exaggerating the fall each time.

languageFocus.thai: คุณชื่ออะไร
languageFocus.notes: คุณ (khun) = polite 'you'. No verb 'to be' needed in Thai.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show each new word as its own triplet card first, then reveal the combined question chunk.
teachingVisuals: Large triplet card for ชื่อ alone, then a second card for อะไร alone.
Combined chunk card for คุณชื่ออะไร with all three parts visible.
teacherCues: Model each word slowly before combining.
Point to each part of the chunk as you say it.

imageSupport.rationale: Abstract vocabulary — text triplets teach better than any image.

sections.heading: Say your name
sections.purpose: Teach คน (person) individually, then teach the response pattern ผม/ฉันชื่อ... (my name is...) and drill name introductions.
spokenNarration: Now you can ask a name. But you need to answer too. First, one more new word.

คน khon. Listen first. คน. This means 'person'. Say it with me: คน. You will hear this word everywhere in Thai — คนไทย means Thai person, คนอังกฤษ means English person. For now, just know คน means person.
To say your own name, use this pattern: ผมชื่อ... phǒm chûue... if you are male. ฉันชื่อ... chǎn chûue... if you are female. ผม and ฉัน both mean 'I' — ผม for men, ฉัน for women.
Let me model it. ผมชื่อ Nine ครับ. My name is Nine. Notice: no word for 'is' — Thai skips it. Just: I + name + your name.
Your turn. Say your own name using ผมชื่อ or ฉันชื่อ. Add ครับ or ค่ะ at the end. Pause and say it now before I continue.
onScreenBullets: ผมชื่อ... | phǒm chûue... | my name is... (male)
ฉันชื่อ... | chǎn chûue... | my name is... (female)
drills: Response-building: I ask คุณชื่ออะไรครับ — now you answer with your real name using ผมชื่อ or ฉันชื่อ. Say it aloud.
Substitution drill: say ผมชื่อ Nine ครับ. Now replace Nine with your own name. Now say it with ฉัน and ค่ะ instead.
Pause-and-produce: someone asks you คุณชื่ออะไร — what do you say? Answer before I show it.

languageFocus.notes: ผม (phǒm) = I/me for male speakers. No copula needed.
ฉัน (chǎn) = I/me for female speakers.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show คน as its own card, then the two name-answer patterns stacked for comparison.
teachingVisuals: Triplet card for คน alone.
Two pattern cards side by side: ผมชื่อ... (male) and ฉันชื่อ... (female).
teacherCues: Model the full sentence with a real name before asking the learner to substitute.
Emphasise that Thai has no 'is' — this is the number one beginner trap here.

imageSupport.rationale: Pattern teaching — the sentence frame itself is the visual anchor.

sections.heading: Where you come from
sections.purpose: Teach มาจาก (come from) and ประเทศ (country) individually, then combine into the origin pattern มาจากประเทศ... (come from country...).
spokenNarration: Now you can say your name. Next: where you come from.
First word: มาจาก maa-jàak. Listen first. มาจาก. This means 'come from'. Say it with me: มาจาก. It is two parts — มา means come, จาก means from — but learn it as one unit: come from.

Second word: ประเทศ bprà-thêet. Listen first. ประเทศ. This means 'country'. Say it with me: ประเทศ. The bpr sound at the start is new — put your lips together like 'b' but release without a puff of air.
Now combine them: มาจากประเทศ... maa-jàak bprà-thêet — come from country... You add the country name at the end.

Full sentence example coming in the next section — first you need the country names. For now, just practise the frame: ผมมาจากประเทศ... phǒm maa-jàak bprà-thêet... Your turn — say มาจากประเทศ three times until it feels smooth.
onScreenBullets: มาจาก | maa-jàak | come from
ประเทศ | bprà-thêet | country
มาจากประเทศ... | maa-jàak bprà-thêet... | come from (country)...
drills: Listen and repeat: มาจาก. Again. ประเทศ. Again. มาจากประเทศ. Again.
Substitution drill: say มาจากประเทศ slowly. Now say ผมมาจากประเทศ with ครับ. Now say ฉันมาจากประเทศ with ค่ะ.
Pause-and-produce: how do you say 'come from country'? Say it before I show the answer. มาจากประเทศ.

languageFocus.notes: มา (come) + จาก (from). Teach as a single unit.

languageFocus.thai: มาจากประเทศ...

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show each word individually first, then the combined origin pattern frame.
teachingVisuals: Triplet card for มาจาก alone, then ประเทศ alone.
Combined pattern card: มาจากประเทศ... with a blank slot for country name.
teacherCues: Model the bpr sound carefully — it trips English speakers.
Show the full sentence with a country name to make the pattern concrete.

imageSupport.rationale: The sentence frame is the teaching focus — no image needed.

sections.heading: Thailand, England, and America
sections.purpose: Teach ไทย (Thailand), อังกฤษ (England), and อเมริกา (America) individually, then use them in full origin sentences with the pattern from section 3.
spokenNarration: You have the pattern. Now let's fill it with country names.
First country: ไทย thai. Listen first. ไทย. This means Thailand or Thai. Say it with me: ไทย. Mid tone — keep your voice flat and even. Easy.
Second country: อังกฤษ ang-grìt. Listen first. อังกฤษ. This means England or English. Say it with me: อังกฤษ. It sounds quite different from the English word — listen again: อังกฤษ ang-grìt.
Third country: อเมริกา à-mee-rí-kaa. Listen first. อเมริกา. This means America. Say it with me: อเมริกา. Closer to the English word, but the stress is different.
Now put them into sentences. ผมมาจากประเทศไทยครับ — I come from Thailand. ฉันมาจากประเทศอังกฤษค่ะ — I come from England. ผมมาจากประเทศอเมริกาครับ — I come from America.
Remember คน from earlier? คนไทย khon thai means Thai person. คนอังกฤษ khon ang-grìt means English person. Your turn — what is คนอเมริกา? Say it before I tell you.
onScreenBullets: อังกฤษ | ang-grìt | England / English
อเมริกา | à-mee-rí-kaa | America
คนไทย | khon thai | Thai person
คนอังกฤษ | khon ang-grìt | English person
drills: Listen and repeat: ไทย, อังกฤษ, อเมริกา. Say each one twice.
Substitution drill: say ผมมาจากประเทศไทยครับ. Now swap ไทย for อังกฤษ. Now swap for อเมริกา.
Pause-and-produce: what is 'Thai person' in Thai? Say it. Now 'English person'. Now 'American person'.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show three country names as triplet cards, then full origin sentences using the pattern.
teachingVisuals: Three triplet cards for ไทย, อังกฤษ, อเมริกา with small flag icons.
Pattern frame: ผม/ฉันมาจากประเทศ___ครับ/ค่ะ with country slot highlighted.
teacherCues: Model อังกฤษ carefully — it surprises English speakers.
Show คน + country to preview the person pattern.

imageSupport.rationale: Small flag icons help anchor country names visually.

sections.heading: Pronunciation: the bp sound
sections.purpose: Train the learner to hear and produce the unaspirated bp/bpr sound in ประเทศ, contrasting it with the aspirated ph sound.
spokenNarration: Let's train your mouth on a new Thai sound. You heard ประเทศ bprà-thêet earlier. That bpr at the start is tricky for English speakers.
In English, you always add a puff of air to p — like in 'pot'. Thai has two kinds of p-sound. One with air: ph, like in ผม phǒm. One without air: bp, like in ประเทศ bprà-thêet.
Try this: hold your hand in front of your mouth. Say 'pot' — you feel air. Now say 'spot' — less air on the p. That 'spot' feeling is closer to Thai bp. Try: bprà-thêet.
Minimal pair: listen carefully. ป่า bpàa means forest. ผ้า phâa means cloth. One has no air puff: bpàa. One has an air puff: phâa. Which one means forest? Say it.
This bp versus ph contrast appears in many Thai words. For now, practise ประเทศ until the bpr feels natural. Say it three more times.
onScreenBullets: ประเทศ | bprà-thêet | country (bp — no air puff)
ป่า | bpàa | forest (bp — no air puff)
ผ้า | phâa | cloth (ph — air puff)
drills: Tone echo: say ประเทศ bprà-thêet three times. Focus on keeping the bp without an air puff.
Minimal pair choice: I say two words — bpàa and phâa. Which one is forest and which is cloth? Point before I tell you.

languageFocus.notes: Unaspirated bp/bpr — no air puff, unlike English p.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show the bp vs ph contrast with clear labels for air puff vs no air puff.
teachingVisuals: Two-column contrast: bp (no air) on left, ph (air) on right.
Minimal pair cards: ป่า bpàa vs ผ้า phâa.
teacherCues: Hold hand in front of mouth to demonstrate air puff difference.
Model each word slowly and clearly.

imageSupport.rationale: Sound contrast is best taught with text labels and teacher modeling.

roleplay.scenario: You meet a new Thai person at a café. Introduce yourself and ask their name and where they are from.

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: สวัสดีครับ

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: ผมชื่อ Mark ครับ คุณชื่ออะไรครับ
lines.translit: phǒm chûue Mark khráp khun chûue à-rai khráp
lines.english: My name is Mark. What is your name?

lines.speaker: New person
lines.thai: ฉันชื่อ Nine ค่ะ คุณมาจากประเทศอะไรคะ
lines.translit: chǎn chûue Nine khâ khun maa-jàak bprà-thêet à-rai khá
lines.english: My name is Nine. What country are you from?

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: ผมมาจากประเทศอังกฤษครับ

lines.speaker: New person
lines.thai: คนอังกฤษคะ

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: ใช่ครับ ผมเป็นคนอังกฤษครับ
lines.translit: châi khráp phǒm bpen khon ang-grìt khráp

lines.speaker: New person
lines.thai: ฉันเป็นคนไทยค่ะ

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: ขอบคุณครับ

recap: How do you ask someone's name in Thai? Say it now.
Say 'my name is...' using your real name.
How do you say 'I come from America'?
What does คนไทย mean?
What is the difference between bp and ph sounds?

targetSounds: unaspirated bp/bpr vs aspirated ph (ป vs พ/ผ)
pronunciationFocus.mouthMapAnchor: Thai bp has no air puff — your lips close and release without pushing air out, like the p in English 'spot'. Thai ph has a strong air puff, like the p in English 'pot'. Hold your hand in front of your mouth to feel the difference.
